meta.name: Aditya Shah
meta.title: Hosting Support Manager & DevOps Engineer
meta.tagline: I scale WordPress infrastructure, secure production systems, and lead teams solving real-world hosting challenges.

hero.description: 10+ years in the WordPress ecosystem — scaling infrastructure, hardening security, and leading high-performance teams at WPMU DEV.

whatIDo.title: Scale WordPress at Millions of Requests
whatIDo.description: Architect and manage hosting infrastructure that keeps hundreds of thousands of WordPress sites online — through traffic spikes, shared resource contention, and edge cases most engineers never see.

whatIDo.title: Harden Compromised Sites Under Pressure
whatIDo.description: Lead malware cleanup and security hardening operations on live, compromised WordPress sites. Identify infection vectors, clean payloads, and implement firewall rules — without taking sites offline.

whatIDo.title: Diagnose and Resolve Production Outages
whatIDo.description: Own the full incident lifecycle — from first alert to postmortem. Debug PHP errors, MySQL deadlocks, OOM kills, and server misconfigurations under real-time customer pressure.

whatIDo.title: Build & Lead High-Performance Support Teams
whatIDo.description: Hire, train, and manage distributed support engineers. Create internal tooling, SOPs, and escalation workflows that reduce response time and increase team autonomy.

whatIDo.title: Automate Internal Ops & Tooling
whatIDo.description: Build automation scripts, Ansible playbooks, and internal dashboards that eliminate repetitive ops tasks and give engineers leverage to work at scale.

whatIDo.title: Optimize for Performance at Scale
whatIDo.description: Profile, benchmark, and tune WordPress sites — from query optimization and caching layers to CDN configuration and PHP-FPM pool tuning.

timeline.title: Started with WordPress
timeline.description: Began hands-on work with WordPress — troubleshooting, customization, and hosting.

timeline.title: First Community Leadership Role
timeline.description: Took on a leadership role in organizing WordPress meetups and events. Began public speaking at local WordPress conferences.

timeline.title: Happiness Engineer
timeline.description: Provided rigorous technical support and troubleshooting for enterprise WooCommerce plugins, helping customers resolve complex conflicts and configuration issues.

timeline.title: Joined WPMU DEV as Hosting Support Engineer
timeline.description: Joined the WPMU DEV hosting team. Rapidly moved from ticket support to complex hosting investigations, malware cleanup, and server-level debugging.

timeline.title: WordCamp Speaker & Organizer
timeline.description: Spoke at WordCamp on WordPress security and hosting best practices. Reached hundreds of developers, designers, and business owners.

timeline.title: Promoted to Senior Support Engineer
timeline.description: Took ownership of high-severity escalations, cross-team coordination, and internal tooling. Mentored junior engineers and contributed to SOPs.

timeline.title: GDG & DevFest Speaker
timeline.description: Delivered talks at GDG events and DevFest conferences on DevOps, automation, and WordPress at scale to audiences of 300+ per event.

timeline.title: Hosting Support Manager
timeline.description: Promoted to team manager. Leading the hosting support division — overseeing hiring, quality, tooling, escalation handling, and customer outcomes.

timeline.title: DevOps Engineer + Manager
timeline.description: Dual role combining team leadership with hands-on DevOps work: Ansible automation, infrastructure observability, incident response, and internal tooling development.

highlights: Lead a distributed team of hosting support engineers across multiple time zones
Own incident response for critical customer-facing hosting outages end-to-end
Perform live malware cleanup and security hardening on compromised WordPress production sites
Build Ansible playbooks and internal tooling to automate repetitive ops workflows
Manage escalations involving PHP, MySQL, Nginx, server configuration, and WordPress core conflicts
Conduct hiring, onboarding, and performance reviews for support engineering roles
Drive internal knowledge base, SOPs, and training programs for the hosting team
Delivered technical support and advanced troubleshooting for a robust suite of WooCommerce plugins
Diagnosed plugin conflicts, theme incompatibilities, and PHP errors for e-commerce stores
Collaborated with developers to report bugs and suggest feature enhancements

community.description: Spoke and helped organize multiple WordCamp events across India. Topics covered WordPress security, hosting architecture, and community growth.
Delivered sessions at Google Developer Group's DevFest events on DevOps, automation, and building developer tooling in the WordPress ecosystem.
Ran hands-on workshops on WordPress security, performance optimization, and hosting management for developers and business owners.
Organized regular WordPress community meetups in Bhopal. Created a consistent learning environment for local WordPress professionals.

blog.title: Debugging WordPress Production Outages: A Field Guide
blog.excerpt: The methodical approach real hosting engineers use to diagnose and resolve outages — from OOM kills to MySQL deadlocks — under customer pressure.

blog.title: WordPress Malware Cleanup Without Downtime
blog.excerpt: How to identify infection vectors, clean malicious payloads, and harden a WordPress site — all without taking it offline or losing data.

blog.title: Scaling WordPress: What Actually Matters
blog.excerpt: Skip the tutorials. Here's what actually bottlenecks WordPress at scale — and the real fixes that move the needle in production environments.

blog.title: Building Ansible Workflows for WordPress Hosting Ops
blog.excerpt: How I use Ansible to automate repetitive hosting operations tasks — from server provisioning to bulk site health checks — saving hours every week.

blog.title: Leading Remote Support Teams That Actually Scale
blog.excerpt: Lessons from managing distributed support engineers across time zones — what works, what fails, and how to build a team with high autonomy.

blog.title: The WordPress Security Checklist I Actually Use
blog.excerpt: Not the generic checklist you've read 100 times. This is what a hosting support manager actually checks when investigating a compromised site.
